explanation_md: `display` takes a `const int&`. For `display(x)`, `n` binds to `x` and prints `9 + 1 = 10`. For `display(4)`, `n` binds to the rvalue `4` (via a compiler-created temporary) and prints `4 + 1 = 5`. Both calls succeed because `const&` accepts both lvalues and rvalues.

prompt_md: Which parameter type allows passing both `myVar` (a named `int`) and the literal `42` to a function that only needs to read the value?
explanation_md: Both pass-by-value (`int n`) and pass-by-const-reference (`const int& n`) accept rvalues like `42`. A non-const lvalue reference (`int& n`) rejects rvalues because it requires a modifiable named object. The difference between a and c is cost: for large types, `const&` avoids a copy; for `int`, either is fine.

options.a: You cannot add `a + b` when they are references
options.c: `int& b` cannot bind to the rvalue `10`; parameter should be `const int& b`

explanation_md: A non-const lvalue reference (`int& b`) can only bind to a modifiable named variable. The literal `10` is an rvalue and has no address, so the compiler rejects the binding. Changing `int& b` to `const int& b` (or `int b`) fixes the error because `const&` and value parameters both accept rvalues.

prompt_md: Why is `const int&` generally preferred over `int&` for a read-only parameter?

options.a: It is faster because it skips the reference binding step
options.b: It allows passing rvalues and literals, and documents that the function will not modify the argument
options.c: It causes the compiler to copy the value into a register for speed
options.d: It prevents the caller's variable from being changed by any other code

explanation_md: `const int&` widens what callers can pass (including literals and temporaries) and signals in the type system that the function only reads the value — no modification possible. It does not affect register use or copy semantics, and it only restricts this particular reference from writing, not the object itself from being changed elsewhere.
